pathophysiology_summary: Acute laryngitis is viral inflammation and oedema of the vocal folds following an upper respiratory infection, producing hoarseness/aphonia, a dry cough and mild odynophagia (largely from repeated throat clearing). It is benign and self-limiting over 1–2 weeks; voice returns as fold oedema settles. Antibiotics are ineffective. The ground-truth reasoning task is to exclude the airway-threatening mimics — epiglottitis and bacterial tracheitis — by screening for stridor, drooling, inability to swallow saliva, tripod positioning and systemic toxicity (all absent here), and to give voice-rest advice (true rest, not whispering, which strains the folds more than soft phonation).

differential.evidence_basis: Coryzal prodrome + hoarseness/aphonia + dry cough is textbook viral laryngitis.
Airway emergency — must screen for drooling, stridor, tripod posture, muffled voice, severe odynophagia, toxicity.
Consider with high fever, toxicity, purulent secretions, stridor — absent here but must be screened.
Causes chronic hoarseness but not an acute febrile/coryzal onset; relevant only if hoarseness persists >2–3 weeks.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Viral laryngitis leads, but epiglottitis/tracheitis must be actively excluded before reassurance.

differential.evidence_basis: No stridor, drooling, dysphagia for saliva, tripod posture or toxicity — airway emergencies excluded. Coryzal prodrome supports viral laryngitis.
Airway red flags absent; remains the escalation target if they develop.
Only if hoarseness persists beyond 2–3 weeks — then laryngoscopy.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative airway screen, self-limiting viral laryngitis leads; manage with voice rest + hydration and an airway safety-net.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; refer for laryngoscopy only if hoarseness persists >2–3 weeks (exclude structural/neoplastic causes).

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final position is self-limiting viral laryngitis for home management.

red_flags.description: Airway obstruction features — stridor, difficulty breathing, drooling/inability to swallow saliva
red_flags.clinical_significance: Suggests epiglottitis/supraglottic obstruction — a time-critical airway emergency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Rapid progression to complete airway obstruction and asphyxiation.

red_flags.description: High fever with systemic toxicity / tripod positioning
red_flags.clinical_significance: Points to epiglottitis or bacterial tracheitis rather than benign viral laryngitis.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating viral laryngitis with antibiotics — ineffective and promotes resistance.
Whispering generates more vocal-fold strain than soft normal phonation; true voice rest (or soft voice) is correct.
Not excluding epiglottitis/tracheitis before reassurance in a patient with acute aphonia.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Oropharyngeal and laryngeal examination / indirect laryngoscopy of the vocal folds
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Home management with voice rest and hydration; call 000 for any stridor, breathing difficulty, drooling or inability to swallow saliva.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Reassuring an acute aphonia without an airway screen risks missing early epiglottitis.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Acute laryngitis is almost always viral and self-limiting; manage with voice rest, hydration and analgesia. Antibiotics are not indicated. Refer for laryngoscopy if hoarseness persists beyond 2–3 weeks.
